questions.question: Impulse is defined as:
questions.explanation: Impulse is the product of force and the time interval over which it acts: $J = F \times t$.

questions.question: A $0.5\text{ kg}$ ball experiences a change in velocity of $12\text{ m/s}$. What is the impulse delivered?
questions.explanation: Impulse $= \Delta p = m \times \Delta v = 0.5 \times 12 = 6.0\text{ N s}$.

questions.question: An impulse of $240\text{ N s}$ is delivered over $0.6\text{ s}$. What is the average force?
A $500\text{ N}$ force delivers an impulse of $1,500\text{ N s}$. How long does the force act?
Airbags in cars reduce injury by:
options: Increasing the force on the passenger
Increasing the time for momentum change
Reducing the passenger's change in momentum
questions.explanation: Airbags extend the time over which the passenger stops. Since $F = \Delta p / t$, a longer time means a smaller force for the same change in momentum.

questions.question: When catching a fast-moving cricket ball, a fielder moves their hands backward upon impact. This reduces the:
questions.explanation: Moving the hands backward increases the stopping time. Since $F = \Delta p / t$, the force is reduced. Impulse and change in momentum stay the same.

questions.question: A $0.2\text{ kg}$ ball hits a wall at $15\text{ m/s}$ and rebounds at $10\text{ m/s}$ in the opposite direction. If the contact time is $0.05\text{ s}$, what is the magnitude of the average force exerted by the wall?
questions.explanation: Initial $p = 0.2 \times 15 = 3.0\text{ kg m/s}$. Final $p = 0.2 \times (-10) = -2.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $\Delta p = 5.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $F = 5.0 / 0.05 = 100\text{ N}$.

questions.question: An impulse of $45\text{ N s}$ changes an object's velocity by $9\text{ m/s}$. What is its mass?
A force-time graph shows a constant force of $200\text{ N}$ acting for $3\text{ s}$. What is the impulse delivered?
questions.explanation: Impulse equals the area under a force-time graph. Area $= F \times t = 200 \times 3 = 600\text{ N s}$.

questions.question: A glass cup and a plastic cup of equal mass are dropped from the same height onto a hard floor. The glass shatters while the plastic bounces. Why?
options: The glass experiences a larger impulse
The glass experiences a larger change in momentum
The glass stops in a shorter time
questions.explanation: Both cups have the same change in momentum. Glass is rigid and stops almost instantly (small $t$), so the force is much larger. Plastic deforms, extending the stopping time and reducing the force.

questions.question: A person pushes against a brick wall with a force of $150\text{ N}$ for $10\text{ s}$. What is the impulse delivered to the wall?
questions.explanation: Impulse $= F \times t = 150 \times 10 = 1,500\text{ N s}$. The wall does not gain momentum because other forces (from the ground) balance the push.

questions.question: A $70\text{ N}$ force acts on a $14\text{ kg}$ object for $2\text{ s}$. If the object started from rest, what is its final velocity?
questions.explanation: Impulse $= 70 \times 2 = 140\text{ N s}$. $\Delta p = m \times v_f = 140$. $v_f = 140 / 14 = 10\text{ m/s}$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the Impulse-Momentum Theorem and its real-world applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review problems involving direction changes and the relationship $F = \Delta p / t$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $\text{Impulse} = F \times t = m \Delta v$ and the effect of time on force.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: impulse definition, the impulse-momentum relationship, and applications like airbags and crumple zones.
